body_txt: An episode of an ongoing show which consists mainly of fragments (clips) of previous episodes, usually with a bare minimum Framing Device . The clips are often grouped together under a theme: highlighting a character's development over the years, relationship between characters, musical interludes , Amusing Injuries , etc. Sometimes there is little rhyme or reason to it all, however, it won't be shown that the events take place in the past, but they are shown as appearing directly one after another. This is a contrast to Stock Footage , which is easily repeatable clips that are treated as a new thing every time. Sometime the episode is entirely a behind-the-scenes endeavor that focus on the highlights of the show, franchise or Hilarious Outtakes , and may be accompanied by a documentary-style voiceover. Other times it's a separate television special. The typical use was to stretch the budget — they utilize footage already shot and only need about 20% new material for the narrative glue, and indeed are one form of a Bottle Episode . They can also be used to stretch the story — when a show's episode order is suddenly expanded after its early episodes prove popular, but the writers don't have enough story to fill quite that many episodes, clip shows are a popular recourse. If they need an extra episode inside a Story Arc , a clip show can be used to sum up a season or storyline as a Recap Episode . The second-to-last episode of a season is especially popular for this; used to conserve the late season's budget so they can blow the wad on a kickass finale. For a time even a Grand Finale may dedicate at least a portion of its runtime to showcasing clips. Because it's focused on the editing a clip show can be made whole cloth when they are localized for other markets, as editing is cheaper and you can use recap segments and voice acting to replace the gaps made when nudity and other risqué content is deleted (occasionally this can even happen before an episode is aired in the original market, as was the case with Tengen Toppa Gurren Lagann 's infamous Hot Springs Episode ). A Frankenslation utilizes very similar methods. There were even theatrical films that served as a clip show, most famously the That's Entertainment! films in the 1970s, as well as Compilation Movies . At one point clip shows were appreciated by viewers as before reruns, syndication, and videotapes / DVDs this was an alternative way for them to revisit the old moments of their shows, or get a glimpse of an episode they missed. In those days, they were less likely to get today's cynical reaction, "They've just done it to save money." When they manifest in adult programming there is usually a little more effort made to make it fit within the canon, even make it an important part of the Myth Arc . But because of easier access of home video and a more discerning audience aware of why they exist their popularity diminished by the late 90's and a more precision Bottle Episode became more popular. Especially as more serialized storytelling became standard (clip shows became impossible to place in the schedule without cluing in audiences on being Out of Order ). They still occasionally emerge in today's TV productions, though mainly in children's programming. Due to gradually becoming a Dead Horse Trope , clip shows became more often parodied than played straight — a common choice is set up a clip show, only for the actual clips to be from stories and events we never actually saw , or be comically off from the ones we did see . A variant on the clip show are the recap or catch-up specials that often precede new-season premieres for arc-heavy series that serve to help new and returning viewers understand the storyline so far. Often, these specials are documentaries, with narration or on-screen introductions by cast members; occasionally, however, the actors appear in character (or, as occurred with the Farscape: Undressed , there was a mixture of both). Another variant is a clip show using footage not from any one particular series, with voice-over narration. These shows are usually factual, like rolling news, Magazine Shows , or nature documentaries, but purely entertaining shows with this format also exist. When previous clips of a single character's line or action are played out in rapid succession (such as Homer's "D'oh" sequence in So It's Come To This: A Simpsons Clip Show ), that's a Fully Automatic Clip Show . Compare this to the use of the Magical Security Cam . For instances of a feature-length film essentially being a Clip Show, see Compilation Movie . The video game equivalent is Megamix Game . See also Recap by Audit .
